agenda_briefs.title: Council to consider 2025 Homeless Count report for Vancouver
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council will consider a report and presentation on the 2025 Homeless Count for Vancouver. This is a proposed item on the council agenda — no decision has been made yet. The report likely provides data on homelessness in the region, which could inform future policies or funding decisions that may affect local businesses, such as those related to public safety, property management, or social services.
agenda_briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail, Professional Services
agenda_briefs.source_body:
1.	2025 Homeless Count – Vancouver
(no speakers per section 7.2(d) of the Procedure By-law)
Report
Presentation
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby 2026 Cultural Grants for Operating & Spaces
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on proposed 2026 Cultural Grants that would provide funding for arts and culture organizations in Burnaby. The grants cover two areas: Operating grants (to help run day-to-day activities) and Cultural Spaces grants (to support buildings or venues used for arts and culture). Because some of the recommendations require a special two-thirds majority vote under Section 206(1) of the Vancouver Charter — a rule that needs extra support from council members — this decision needs more than a simple majority to pass. The proposal has not been approved yet and will be decided at an upcoming council meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — if approved, this could affect funding for your arts or culture organization in Burnaby.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
2.	2026 Cultural Grants (Balance of Operating, Cultural Spaces)
Recommendations A to C, E and G require at least 2/3 affirmative votes of all Council members under Section 206(1) of the Vancouver Charter
Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Vancouver Community Sport Event Grants for Spring 2026
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposal to award grants to community sport events happening in Vancouver during the spring of 2026. This is part of the Vancouver Community Sport Event Grant program. The recommendation requires at least two-thirds of all council members to vote 'yes' to pass, as per Section 206(1) of the Vancouver Charter (a special rule for certain council decisions). If approved, the grants would help local organizations host sporting events that benefit the community.
agenda_briefs.action_required: No action needed from you right now. Watch for the council's decision to see if your local sport event could apply for future grants.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
3.	Vancouver Community Sport Event Grants – Spring 2026 Intake
Recommendation A requires at least 2/3 affirmative votes of all Council members under Section 206(1) of the Vancouver Charter
Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby property tax distribution for 2026
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on how to divide the total property tax levy among different classes of properties (like homes, businesses, and industry) for the 2026 tax year. This decision will determine how much of the overall tax burden falls on commercial and residential properties. A proposed bylaw would set the tax rates for each class. The outcome directly affects how much property tax your business will pay next year.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — this will set your 2026 property tax rate.
agenda_briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail, Professional Services
agenda_briefs.source_body:
4.	2026 Property Taxation: Distribution of Property Tax Levy
4a. Budget Resolution
4b. Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby 2025 property tax exemptions for non-profits
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a report that recommends granting property tax exemptions for 2025 to certain non-profit organizations in Burnaby. A property tax exemption means the organization does not have to pay property tax on the land or building it uses. This decision would affect which groups get the break and how much tax revenue the city gives up. The report lists the specific organizations and properties being considered for the exemption.
agenda_briefs.action_required: No action required from you at this time. Watch for the council decision to see if your property or business is affected.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
5.	2025 Property Tax Exemptions
(no speakers per section 7.2(d) of the Procedure By-law)
Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Coastal Flood Management Policy
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed Coastal Flood Management Policy. This policy would create new rules for building and development in areas of Burnaby that are at risk of flooding from the ocean. If approved, property owners in these flood-risk zones may face new requirements for building elevations, flood-proofing, and insurance. The goal is to protect properties from rising sea levels and storm surges. The report is up for discussion and a vote at an upcoming council meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Check if your property is in a proposed flood-risk zone and attend the public hearing to have your say.
agenda_briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Insurance & Finance
agenda_briefs.source_body:
6.	Coastal Flood Management Policy
Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Council to vote on Vancouver Heritage Foundation Board annual report
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a recommendation (Recommendation B) related to the Vancouver Heritage Foundation Board's 2025 Annual Report. This recommendation requires at least two-thirds of all council members to vote in favour, as stated in Section 206(1) of the Vancouver Charter. The Vancouver Charter is the city's main governing law. The decision is not yet final — council will debate and vote on it at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.	Vancouver Heritage Foundation Board – Annual Report 2025
Recommendation B requires at least 2/3 affirmative votes of all Council members under Section 206(1) of theVancouver Charter
Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby council to vote on public plaza alcohol sites for 2026-2029
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposal to choose which public plazas in Burnaby will allow people to drink alcohol in public from 2026 to 2029. This is part of the Alcohol Consumption in Public Plazas Program. If approved, the program would let adults drink alcohol in certain outdoor public spaces during specific hours and dates. The decision has not been made yet — council will discuss and vote on the list of sites at an upcoming meeting. Business owners near proposed plazas should pay attention, as this could affect foot traffic, noise, and local business atmosphere.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council meeting date and consider attending or submitting comments if your business is near a proposed plaza site.

key_dates.label: Council vote on proposed plaza alcohol sites for 2026-2029

agenda_briefs.source_body:
8.	Sites for 2026-2029 Alcohol Consumption in Public Plazas Program
Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby seeks grant to speed up development approvals
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider applying for a provincial grant under the Local Government Development Approvals Program. If approved, the funding would help the city hire more staff and upgrade technology to process building and development permits faster. This is not a new rule or fee — it is a request for money to improve the city's approval system. A decision on whether to apply is expected at the next council meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: No action needed now. Watch for council's decision — faster approvals could affect your project timelines.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
9.	Funding Application to Local Government Development Approvals Program
Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to amend housing rezoning conditions
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on miscellaneous amendments that would change the conditions attached to rezoning approvals for various housing projects in Burnaby. These proposed changes could affect what developers must provide or agree to when they get permission to rezone land for housing. The exact details of the amendments are not specified in the agenda item, but the decision could impact future housing developments and the conditions they must meet.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision and review the full amendment details once released — this could affect your development project's rezoning conditions.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
10.	Miscellaneous Amendments Concerning Various Housing Rezoning Conditions
(no speakers per section 7.2(b) of the Procedure By-law)
Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Text amendment to CD-1 (839) bylaw for 5590 Victoria Drive and 2005 East 40th Avenue
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed text amendment to the Comprehensive Development (CD-1) Bylaw No. 13493 for the properties at 5590 Victoria Drive and 2005 East 40th Avenue. A CD-1 bylaw is a special zoning rule that allows a specific development plan for a site. This amendment would change the rules for what can be built or done on these two properties. The decision has not been made yet — it is up for approval at a future council meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision date — this amendment could affect development plans for these properties.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: CD-1 Rezoning at 4088 Granville Street, Burnaby
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed rezoning bylaw for 4088 Granville Street. This would change the property's zoning to a Comprehensive Development (CD-1) district, which is a custom zoning category that allows for specific uses and building designs not permitted under standard zoning rules. The draft bylaw amends the city's main Zoning and Development By-law. A decision has not been made yet — this is an upcoming item on the council agenda.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision date — this rezoning could affect nearby property values or development plans.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Rezoning for 3803-3823 West 10th Ave and 2553 Highbury St
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on a proposed rezoning for properties at 3803-3823 West 10th Avenue and 2553 Highbury Street. If approved, the project would change the land-use rules (called a CD-1 rezoning) to allow a new development on these sites. The proposal also includes amendments to the Sign By-law. This is not yet approved — Council will make a final decision at a future meeting.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Rezoning at 1305-1325 West 13th Avenue in Burnaby
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would rezone the properties at 1305-1325 West 13th Avenue. A 'rezoning' means changing the official rules about what can be built or done on that land. This is a first step — the draft bylaw is up for approval at a council meeting. agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby amenity cost charge bylaw and density bonus updates
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to consider proposed updates to the Amenity Cost Charge (ACC) bylaw and the Density Bonus and Inclusionary Zoning rules. An Amenity Cost Charge is a fee the city charges developers to help pay for community amenities like parks, community centres, and libraries that are needed because of new development. The Density Bonus allows developers to build more units than normally allowed in exchange for providing community benefits. The proposed updates would change how these charges and bonuses are calculated and applied. Council will also consider changes related to Bill 16, a provincial law that affects how municipalities can use density bonuses and inclusionary zoning (rules that require affordable housing in new developments). A required public consultation on these proposed changes is part of the process before any final decision is made.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the public consultation notice — this is your chance to comment on proposed changes to development fees and density rules.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
16.	2026 Financing Growth Update – Required Consultation for Amenity Cost Charge By law and Bill 16 Density Bonus and Inclusionary Zoning Updates
Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Bylaw to assess property for Trounce Alley and Blood Alley Square maintenance costs
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would charge property owners in the Trounce Alley and Blood Alley Square area for the 2025 maintenance costs of those public spaces. This is a 'local improvement' project, meaning the costs are shared among the properties that benefit directly from the upkeep. The bylaw would allow the city to collect these costs through property taxes. This is not yet approved — Council will decide at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council meeting date and consider attending to have your say on the proposed maintenance charges.
agenda_briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail, Food Service
agenda_briefs.source_body: 8.	A By-law to assess real property to defray 2025 maintenance costs for the Trounce Alley and Blood Alley Square Local Improvement Project

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby bylaw to assess property costs for East Hastings parking project
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would charge certain property owners in the East Hastings Street area for the costs of a shared parking project in 2025. The bylaw would 'assess real property' — meaning it would add a special fee or tax on specific properties to pay for the collective parking project. This is not yet approved; it is up for discussion and a vote at an upcoming council meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council meeting date and consider attending or submitting comments if you own property on East Hastings Street.
agenda_briefs.source_body: 7.	A By-law to assess real property to defray 2025 costs for the East Hastings Street Collective Parking Project
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Housing agreement for 320-360 West 2nd Avenue
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would create a Housing Agreement for the property at 320-360 West 2nd Avenue. A Housing Agreement is a legal contract between the city and a property owner that locks in how the building will be used — for example, requiring a certain number of affordable or rental units. This is a proposed decision only; it has not been approved yet.
agenda_briefs.source_body: 6.	A By-law to enact a Housing Agreement for 320-360 West 2nd Avenue
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Amend housing agreement for 450-496 Prior Street, 550 Malkin Ave, 1002 Station St
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would amend an existing Housing Agreement for three properties in Burnaby: 450-496 Prior Street, 550 Malkin Avenue, and 1002 Station Street. A Housing Agreement is a legal contract between the city and a property owner that sets rules about how the housing is used — for example, keeping units affordable or limiting rent increases. This proposed change would update the terms of that agreement. The decision has not been made yet.
agenda_briefs.source_body: 5.	A By-law to authorize the amendment of a Housing Agreement Authorized by By-law No. 14507 (450 – 496 Prior Street, 550 Malkin Avenue and 1002 Station Street)
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby council to vote on zoning amendment at 1700-1750 West 75th Avenue
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would amend the existing Comprehensive Development (CD-1) zoning for the properties at 1700-1750 West 75th Avenue. A 'bylaw' is a local law passed by the city council. This amendment would change the rules for what can be built or done on that specific piece of land. The decision has not been made yet — council will discuss and vote on it at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council meeting date and attend or submit your comments if you own property or a business near this location.
agenda_briefs.source_body:

agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Rezone 1710-1730 East Pender Street to CD-1
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would rezone the properties at 1710-1730 East Pender Street to a Comprehensive Development (CD-1) zone. A CD-1 zone is a custom zoning category that allows for a specific development plan, rather than following the standard rules for that area. This decision is on the agenda for an upcoming council meeting, but has not yet been approved.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council meeting date and attend the public hearing to have your say on this proposed rezoning.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
3.	A By-law to amend Zoning and Development By-law No. 3575 to rezone an area to CD-1 (1710-1730 East Pender Street)
(Councillor Maloney, Orr and Mayor Sim will need to review the proceedings to be eligible to vote)
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Rezone 6486 Chester Street to CD-1 in Burnaby
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would rezone the property at 6486 Chester Street to a Comprehensive Development (CD-1) zone. A CD-1 zone is a custom zoning category that allows for a specific development plan, rather than following the standard rules for that area. This is a proposed change only — it has not been approved yet. Council will consider the bylaw at an upcoming meeting. Councillors Bligh, Fry, and Mayor Sim must review the proceedings before they are eligible to vote on this matter.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council meeting date and attend or submit comments if you own property or have interests near 6486 Chester Street.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
2.	A By-law to amend Zoning and Development By-law No. 3575 to rezone an area to CD-1 (6486 Chester Street)
(Councillors Bligh, Fry and Mayor Sim will need to review the proceedings to be eligible to vote)
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Rezone 1302-1318 East 12th Avenue to CD-1
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would rezone the properties at 1302-1318 East 12th Avenue to a Comprehensive Development (CD-1) zone. A CD-1 zone is a custom zoning category that allows for a specific development plan, rather than following the standard rules for that area. This is a first reading of the bylaw — it has not been approved yet. Councillors Klassen, Maloney, Montague, Orr and the Mayor must review the proceedings before they are eligible to vote on it.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the public hearing date — this is your chance to speak for or against the proposed rezoning.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
1.	A By-law to amend Zoning and Development By-law No. 3575 to rezone an area to CD-1 (1302 - 1318 East 12th Avenue)
(Councillors Klassen, Maloney, Montague, Orr and Mayor Sim will need to review the proceedings to be eligible to vote)

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Rezoning for 888-896 Cambie Street and 111-191 Smithe Street
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on a proposed rezoning for properties at 888-896 Cambie Street and 111-191 Smithe Street. This is a 'CD-1 Rezoning', which means the city is considering changing the official zoning rules for these specific lots to allow a different type of development. The decision has not been made yet — Council will discuss and vote on the proposal at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Attend the public hearing or submit written comments to Burnaby City Council before the vote to share your support or concerns about this rezoning.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
3.	CD-1 Rezoning: 888-896 Cambie Street and 111-191 Smithe Street
Referral Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: CD-1 Rezoning for 888 West 8th Avenue in Burnaby
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed rezoning for the property at 888 West 8th Avenue. If approved, the site would be rezoned to a Comprehensive Development (CD-1) district, which is a special zoning category that allows for a mix of uses not normally permitted under standard zoning. This proposal is still under consideration and has not been finalized. A referral report has been prepared for council to review before making a decision.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — this rezoning could affect nearby property values and development plans.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
2.	CD-1 Rezoning: 888 West 8th Avenue
Referral Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Fast-track rental housing and small hotels in new Burnaby commercial district
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on a proposal to create a new 'Mixed-Use Commercial District' that would fast-track approvals for rental housing and small hotels. If approved, this would change the rules for certain areas, making it faster and easier for developers to build rental apartments and small hotels without going through the usual lengthy rezoning process. The goal is to get more rental housing built quickly. This is a proposed change only — it has not been approved yet.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the public hearing date — this is your chance to tell Council if you support or oppose the new district rules.
agenda_briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Hotel & Accommodation, Landlord & Property Mgmt
agenda_briefs.source_body:
1.	Fast-Tracking Rental Housing and Small Hotels – New Mixed-Use Commercial District
Referral Report
